task: I’ve got a few practical papers to get in order, and I want to make sure I’m not missing anything important before I set them aside. Could you help me make a simple checklist for what to review, what to keep together, and what to handle first?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple, plain checklist you can use before setting papers aside.

## 1) Review first
- [ ] Read each paper once
- [ ] Note the **name**
- [ ] Note the **date**
- [ ] Note any **address, place, or relationship**
- [ ] Mark anything that looks important or unclear
- [ ] Put aside anything that needs later checking

## 2) Keep together
- [ ] Papers from the **same person**
- [ ] Papers from the **same year or date range**
- [ ] Letters that belong to the **same set or exchange**
- [ ] Papers that mention the **same family event**
- [ ] Duplicate copies, if you want to keep them together for now

## 3) Handle first
- [ ] Old or fragile papers
- [ ] Handwritten letters
- [ ] Papers with names you may want to look up later
- [ ] Papers with dates that help place other items
- [ ] Anything loose, torn, or hard to read

## 4) Set aside for later
- [ ] Items you know are important
- [ ] Items you need to scan or copy
- [ ] Items with missing dates
- [ ] Items with repeated names from different years
- [ ] Items you are unsure about

## 5) Simple note to make for each item
- [ ] Name:
- [ ] Date:
- [ ] Type:
- [ ] Notes:
- [ ] Box/folder:

If you want, I can turn this into a very plain one-page checklist format you can print and use by hand.

user_simulator.text:
That’s fine. Clear enough, and not fussy. I’d use it.

I do like the part about keeping names, dates, and who it belongs to right at the top. That’s usually what matters when you’re sorting old papers. And putting the fragile ones first makes sense too — no point handling those twice.

I’ve done this sort of thing before, with family papers. The hard part is not the sorting, it’s deciding what to leave for later without letting it pile up again. This keeps it practical.

So yes, this works.
